skill.name: unit-testing-test-generate
skill.description: Generate comprehensive, maintainable unit tests across languages with coverage analysis and edge case focus.
skill.summary: Creates unit test plans and examples for common language frameworks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and system reconnaissance patterns. Most reported patterns are false positives from Markdown code fences, TypeScript template strings, and words such as description or describe. One legitimate concern remains: the coverage analyzer example runs a caller-provided test command with subprocess.run, so users should review commands before execution.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Provided Test Command Execution Example
medium_findings.description: The coverage analyzer example calls subprocess.run with a test_command parameter. The code does not use shell=True, but executing a user-provided test command can still run arbitrary project commands if copied directly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The external command call is visible and semantically tied to coverage analysis. It appears legitimate for test tooling, but the command value is caller-provided and needs user review.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Matches Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby shell backtick locations are Markdown code fences or TypeScript template strings used to generate test text. No evidence found of Ruby shell execution or hidden command execution at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context shows documentation examples and TypeScript interpolation syntax. There is no shell context, Ruby code, or executable backtick command.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography locations map to plain words such as description and describe. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA-1, or another weak cryptographic algorithm being used.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact line context contains prose or test framework function names, not cryptographic APIs. This is a clear substring-based false positive.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Matches Are Test Name False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported reconnaissance locations are test case names about expected results and invalid input. No evidence found of system probing, environment discovery, or host reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are inside a TypeScript array of generated Jest test case descriptions. The text is not connected to operating system commands or data collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a Python example that runs a coverage command through subprocess.run. This is a valid testing pattern but should only run trusted commands in trusted repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess.run call is direct and easy to verify. The surrounding context is coverage analysis, so the risk is command execution misuse rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Writing complete unit tests is slow when code spans several languages and frameworks. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create structured tests, mocks, and coverage-focused scenarios.
actual_capabilities: Identifies Python functions, classes, arguments, decorators, docstrings, and complexity from source structure.
Drafts pytest test suites with happy path, empty input, invalid input, and exception scenarios.
Creates Jest-style test suites for JavaScript and TypeScript functions from names and parameters.
Outlines React Testing Library tests for rendering, props, state, and user interaction.
Describes coverage gap analysis and targeted test generation from coverage output.
Generates mock object and fixture patterns using unittest.mock concepts.
limitations: It is a guidance skill with examples, not a packaged test generator application.
Generated assertions need review against real business rules and expected values.
Coverage analysis requires running project tests and depends on the selected command output.
It focuses on unit tests and does not replace integration or end-to-end testing.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap Tests For Legacy Modules
use_cases.description: Generate first-pass unit tests for modules that have little or no current test coverage.

use_cases.title: Standardize Frontend Test Patterns
use_cases.description: Create consistent Jest and React Testing Library scenarios for component behavior and props.

use_cases.title: Target Coverage Gaps Before Release
use_cases.description: Use coverage reports to focus new tests on uncovered files, branches, and functions.

prompt_templates.title: Generate Basic Unit Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to generate unit tests for the selected file. Include happy path, invalid input, and edge case scenarios.

prompt_templates.title: Create Framework-Specific Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request with a known stack.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate tests for this module using the project framework. Match the existing pytest, Jest, or React Testing Library style.

prompt_templates.title: Add Mocks And Fixtures
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for code with dependencies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create unit tests with mocks and fixtures for external dependencies. Keep assertions specific to observable behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Close Coverage Gaps
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request using a coverage report.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the coverage gaps and generate targeted unit tests for the uncovered branches, errors, and boundary cases.

output_examples.input: A Python service module with public functions and one class.
output_examples.output: A pytest test plan with fixtures, success cases, empty input cases, invalid input cases, and exception handling coverage.

output_examples.input: A React component that renders props and handles a button click.
output_examples.output: A React Testing Library outline covering render behavior, visible text, prop updates, and user interaction.

output_examples.input: A coverage report showing uncovered functions in several files.
output_examples.output: A prioritized list of missing scenarios and targeted unit tests to improve coverage.

best_practices: Review generated tests against real expected behavior before committing them.
Run generated tests in a trusted repository and inspect any proposed test command first.
Prefer specific assertions over checks that only confirm a result exists.
anti_patterns: Do not accept generated wildcard imports or broad mocks without review.
Do not use the skill to replace integration or end-to-end testing needs.
Do not run coverage commands from untrusted input or unknown repositories.

faq.question: Which test frameworks does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It includes guidance for pytest, Jest, React Testing Library, JUnit, and Go testing patterns.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready tests automatically?
faq.answer: It creates strong drafts, but developers must verify expected values and business logic.

faq.question: Can it improve coverage reports?
faq.answer: It can help target uncovered files and functions when you provide coverage output.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: The skill includes a coverage example that can run a test command. Review commands before execution.

faq.question: Can it mock external dependencies?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests fixtures and mock object patterns for dependencies that should not run in unit tests.

faq.question: Is it suitable for compliance-only test suites?
faq.answer: No. The skill states that hand-written tests may be required for compliance workflows.
